chunk_summary: This section differentiates determinant-driven stunting from non-nutritional short stature in clinical settings, emphasizing auxology, history, and targeted investigations to guide diagnosis and management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical distinction between determinant-driven stunting and non-nutritional short stature.
Learn to use auxology, growth velocity, and history patterning for differential diagnosis.
Identify key features of determinant-driven stunting, endocrine causes, and chronic systemic diseases.
Develop appropriate management plans based on the underlying cause of short stature.
key_facts: Stunting in India is predominantly determinant-driven, often linked to early onset within the first 1000 days of life.
Endocrine causes of short stature typically show preserved or increased weight-for-height with delayed bone age.
Chronic systemic diseases often present with weight faltering preceding height faltering and poor appetite.
Measurement confirmation and plotting are the first steps in evaluating short stature.
Determinant-driven stunting requires a multi-domain approach, including diet quality, infection prevention, and maternal health.
reference_quote: A child tracking a low centile with normal growth velocity and family history may represent familial short stature or constitutional delay, while centile crossing, low growth velocity, disproportion, or systemic symptoms increase concern for pathology.
